speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you OK in the kitchen?

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh huh.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Just wanted to make sure that you're comfortable. That if-- This is reel 4 with Alex Reifer, reel 4.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. One more time, say it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is reel 4 with Alex Reifer, reel 4. When we finished reel 3, you were just about to explain the anti-Semitism about one person in particular. And if you remember his name, could you tell us?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the army, which I was in charge in this particular time, I was [NON-ENGLISH] from this particular part. I got 45 people under me. I got several people which was anti-Semitism. And he was from the, from the, the people, which I-- I knew everybody. I got all the writing from who he comes, who he-- he was from the Arabs descent. One guy, his name was-- a terrible guy-- was Shchepetkin, the name. I will never forget. He want to kill me twice. But he didn't. He forgot. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he forget?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I hit him before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you a question--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --regarding your-- the elevation. How did you rise up in the ranks as--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the school. And I was-- I was learning. And I was mostly, I think, smart as a, as a lot of different people. And after that, I was going to school. I study all the time. And the other people didn't study. They slept.

speaker: interviewer
text: So really--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I study.

speaker: interviewer
text: So really, all of the seeds of your life were sown from having gone to school when you were three years old.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right, to a certain way. Also, I, I learn-- I learn a lot of Judo and karate, and I-- self defense. I'm an expert. Maybe today, I am already over 36 years old. Then I'm-- why are you laughing? Then I'm a little over. Then I, I, I'm still OK. This I can tell you. I'm still OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm good with knife throwing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did--

speaker: interviewee
text: Very good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you learn these skills?

speaker: interviewee
text: I learned the skills also part in the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the Soviet Union?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, part in the army, yeah. And I learned by big people, very big-- famous.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like--

speaker: interviewee
text: Famous, famous, famous Judo artists, you know, all kind. I-- we, we-- they bring us-- they bring in these people from Moscow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have it written down. I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is it in that chair?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's right, maybe here, too, also, yeah. And we learn a lot of tricks, very interesting tricks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you another question regarding your-- the way you rose in the ranks. So I'm assuming everything that you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I will explain you again one more thing, why I'm rosing fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't say to anybody. But I will tell you again. To rose in the Russian army, to be somebody, you must join the Communist party. I didn't. I joined-- I was young. I would join the Communist young party, [NON-ENGLISH] they call them. Until you joined, you go through the interview, you-- not like you interview. But they interview you 10 times every week, till they let you in to [NON-ENGLISH]. And I was in [NON-ENGLISH]. And I give you my number, if you want to. I never give to anybody. The number in Russian is [RUSSIAN.] I will explain you in another, another language. [RUSSIAN], 143, 338, 46. And-- and this-- when I received-- this is a number only. But when I received the book, this is the number which I got. A book-- I received a book, the [NON-ENGLISH], of the [NON-ENGLISH] party. And this, I got open doors, complete open doors.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this a--

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to meetings with big company, with big officers. And this, this, this only, in the front, in the army, you can rise. If you're not a commie, you couldn't rise. Impossible, you couldn't do it. They did not let you into school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were fully aware--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that in order to protect your hide--

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not a communist. I'm telling you right away, OK? [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But in order to survive--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you understood what the rules of the game--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. I knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so whatever the rules, whatever it took--

speaker: interviewee
text: I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: To play the-- OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And this bring me up. And I, I, I was very big in the future. I was, I was not a small man in the future. Before, I was a very small man. [LAUGHS] But after that, I was very big. And they got respect for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: I need to ask you yet another question. This isn't about the army. I mean, we're going to go back and forth here.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Were you aware of or understood or even knew about treatment of Jews in other places in the Soviet Union, like those Jews who had fled Poland like yourself, who maybe had gone on to central Russia or to Siberia? Did, did you come in contact with anyone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you weren't aware of what was--

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew. I knew that this [INAUDIBLE]--

speaker: interviewer
text: Semitism.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anti-Semitism in Russia, because I feeled it. But to know about the Novosibirsk or Novosibirsk [NON-ENGLISH], or this, I, I knew it is. But I didn't knew the, the people which was something like that. I knew it is, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Also, because I've never talked to anyone from this side, you know, from, from, from the Russian side, were you aware-- did you know about any of the concentration camps or the killing camps, maybe the, the death camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you know by 1942?

speaker: interviewee
text: We know about Auschwitz. We know about Birkenau. We know about Dachau. We know.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you-- you-- so you were aware-- you, yourself, knew--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --Treblinka, Belzec, Majdanek.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm, mm-hm.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, in the winter of '42, then, because you became an officer when?

speaker: interviewee
text: I became an officer in '43.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So it's still--

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] is not officer. [NON-ENGLISH] is before officer.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So were you at the Battle of Stalingrad?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you? What were you doing during the Battle of Stalingrad?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fighting.

speaker: interviewer
text: A soldier?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, not a soldier. I was already higher. I was already a kapo. I was already higher.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you higher?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had two stripes already.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does that-- what would that translate?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before [NON-ENGLISH] is two stripes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you talk about the Battle of Stalingrad a little bit? Would that be-- is there, is-- would you--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not-- for me it's not interested. The battle of Berlin was more interested. And the, and the finish the war was more interested than the Battle of Stalingrad. It was in the paper. It was very big, the Battle of Stalingrad, you see. But for professionals, it was not, not big.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we lose too many people by Stalingrad, for in the, the-- which a lot of time at meetings, I criticize. I mean, I stand up. And I criticize.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was big officers, polkovnik, you know, big officers. I criticize, why did you lost so many? Why didn't you organize before they going? Why do-- why do you bring in people which are not professional soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: They bring in people from different part of the world. They threw them in like, like, like cattle. And it was no good. I criticize a lot. I only criticize in the time when I knew I will be a [NON-ENGLISH] do it. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Speaking of [NON-ENGLISH], how did they interrogate you? You said they interviewed you, like, 10 times a week.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Every-- they ask me, what my family was doing, what things? I didn't tell them the truth. I tell them I made up my mind what I told them. And this was the way I told them. Just, I hold it till the last minute. I didn't change anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what were the things they asked you? And how did you respond?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- they asked me what my father was doing. I said my father was a working man, no store, nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about religion? Did they ask you about religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. Religion didn't-- and if, if you apply for [NON-ENGLISH], they didn't ask any religion, because you wouldn't be-- you're not religious. If you, if you tried to get in to this particular part, they didn't ask this question.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your relationships like with other officers, Jewish or-- the officers I'm talking, not the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Some officers, there was also anti-Semitism. But they respect me, because I, I was a top-- I mean, even if I was small, I mean, in, in rank, I was a top man.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about relationships with other people? I mean, you must have been extremely lonely, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: I never was lonely.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you isolated? Were you isolated because-- what about your friend, Leon Klappholtz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't see him. When we, when we part, I didn't see him at all later on. I was looking for him in Israel, because they told me he is-- I couldn't find him.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you have-- what kind of camaraderie did you have with the other officers, if any?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got friends, officers friends, yeah. I got officers also, a couple officers Jewish people. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You know how in--

speaker: interviewee
text: I got-- I got. I'm sorry. I interrupt you.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got a, a medal, won a, a big medal, I got because officer [NON-ENGLISH] Kuznetsov, he was Jewish. And he, he was going in a place which he don't need to, to go. And it was all mines. And he got in in this particular place-- it was, it was a special mark that he shouldn't go. I mean, nobody should go. He did. I don't know. Maybe he was drunk. I don't know. And, and he blow up. And I was the person-- I was not far from there. A lot of people was afraid to go in. He was still alive. I got in.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I'm, I'm an expert, how. I, I, I was in-- and, and I was in, not in going like that. I was moving. And I, I checked.

speaker: interviewer
text: But, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: And I took, I took him out.

speaker: interviewer
text: But how did you move in? Can you describe that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: I move in-- you see, when you put mines, you see a point on the mines all the time, because if somebody want take off the mines, he know where he put the mine. And I was going between the mines.

speaker: interviewer
text: But were you crawling?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I was crawling, yeah. I was crawling. Not only crawling, I was-- it took me-- it took me a long time till I got to him. And it took me a long time till I got him out. I got him out. And I received a big [RUSSIAN], a, a decoration.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it called, the decoration?

speaker: interviewee
text: [RUSSIAN], one of the biggest [RUSSIAN], before [RUSSIAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: I speak no Russian.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is very hard for me. Could you say that again?

speaker: interviewee
text: [RUSSIAN]. It's a very big [RUSSIAN]. You don't have too many generals get this [RUSSIAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: By this point, what, what, what rank were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was already a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: We don't normally ask questions like I'm asking you, because usually people tell their stories, and-- but you have such specific-- we have such specific information we, we must retrieve from you, essentially--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: --that I-- I'm interrupting you more than normal.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's do this. So why don't you tell me-- because now I think we can leave some questions behind. I have other questions. But I think they'll come later on. Why don't you tell me after you rescued this officer, whose name I cannot--

speaker: interviewee
text: Major Kuznetsov, a major, a major.

speaker: interviewer
text: This major. What year was this and what--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was [NON-ENGLISH]. He was a big, big officer.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in a large--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this in a large battle? Or was this--

speaker: interviewee
text: Was not in a battle at all, because we was on the front. And we mined-- we put mines-- I didn't put it. But we have special people which belong to the mine group. And they put mines that the Germans, when they want to come over on this side, they blow up. That's all what we do. This the mine people especially.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, I have kind of a question that-- with the front, with you here, and the Germans here, was there-- could you hear each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah, not hear. You see, we-- between-- the distance between the German line and, and the Russian line is different distances. Some distance is 10 yards. And some distance is 25 yards.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's different how you make the line. You understand? Some distance is farther.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and in the line, in the line, the, the real soldiers, the regular soldiers, stay in this line. And in the line is special bunkers on the side, which the soldiers, if he is not on patrol, he sleeps there. And our people are behind the line. Let me see, like we and [NON-ENGLISH] is, [NON-ENGLISH] is reconnaissance groups. We stay normally with the [NON-ENGLISH]. For [NON-ENGLISH] means-- [NON-ENGLISH] is a, a big office from the commander from the [NON-ENGLISH]. From the-- a [NON-ENGLISH] is a, is a-- higher the battalion. Each [NON-ENGLISH] has three battalions. And each division has three [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] is-- in English, I forgot already. But I will remind you-- I'll remind so I give you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like headquarters? Like a-- like--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is headquarters from the, from the-- from this particular group. And we are behind, we behind the front line normally a mile. But what we do, my people, the reconnaissance people, walking mostly in the first lines, not all of them. Whenever we have work to do, we make plans. We sketch up which ways to go behind the German lines, and how to catch the Germans. We calling it-- we catching is [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] in Russian is a tongue. [NON-ENGLISH] is a tongue. In Russian, we say, we will catch [NON-ENGLISH]. We will pick. We go down there. And we catch somebody, because we need that the person be interviewed from me or from somebody else, and where the stop is and where this is and how many people he have and when they came, everything. And this we-- this is our operation. This is [NON-ENGLISH]. We are organizing the catching the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get--

speaker: interviewee
text: I am an expert. I can, I can, I can, I can pick, pick her up in five minutes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get-- [LAUGHTER]. How did you get from the Russian front, from the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I will tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to the other side?

speaker: interviewee
text: I will tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: We, we make plans. And then we have special-- special [INAUDIBLE], special-- not light up, but big ones. And we looking-- big one, very big one.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean field glasses.

speaker: interviewee
text: Field glasses. And we look. And we see all the movements. And what kind of people staying down there, when they change the hours, which they go in, and another one comes in. We check the mines, where the mines are. Or there's no mines. We-- before we do our, our job, before we do something, we know-- not 100%, because a lot of the time I lost a lot of people, too. But we know about 90%. We know what we're doing. And when we catch, we don't, we don't-- when we go in to the German side already [INAUDIBLE], is a group of people which goes in. The group of people are professionals. It's four people, four people. They are professional in Judo, in karate, in knife throwing, in clubs, everything. And they have only a gun. They don't have machine guns. We have machine guns, all of them around. And then this four people doing the job-- now this is the four they're calling it [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] is the people which catching people. That's all what they're doing, nothing else. [NON-ENGLISH] is people which is cut out, a left cut and a right cut. The left cut and right cut is normally between five and six people each side, which they do nothing. Only they protect this four people. They don't let in anybody. If something happened, they fight him away. After that, we have [NON-ENGLISH]. [NON-ENGLISH] is a group which is behind. And we cover up all of them. And this is-- whenever I go for a big job-- I did, I don't go anymore. But whenever I did go to a big job, I made a sketch how to do it and which way to do it. And I'm the person which is in charge, nobody else. I don't care it's a general. He must listen to me. Or he's a major. It's not important. I am the top man. And this is the way I make the [INAUDIBLE]. And normally, we have a, a group, 140 people. [NON-ENGLISH] they're calling it. Everything is automats. And the protectors-- then I, I told the, the commander. He is a major. And I tell him I need 28 people or 50 people or 40 people. It's different how far we doing. We doing small, I need less people. And they are behind us to cover us if something happen. And we, we did organize very good. Yeah, I was one of the top professionals. And when it was meetings from our group in the [NON-ENGLISH] division or [NON-ENGLISH] army, I was speaking. I was the top man.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] Did you, yourself-- did you infiltrate the Germans' side?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I-- yeah, all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were physically on the German side?

speaker: interviewee
text: 98%, oh, yeah. I, I, I run this place. I, I didn't tell him to do it. I tell him to do it. But I run the place. I was with them [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you ever frightened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Frightened, frightened, I, I, I was never frightened. Why should I be frightened? I was never frightened.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about interrogations of Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: We have got-- we have got grenades with us. I got four all the time. Something happen, I blow myself up. That's over. It's no use to, to, to be and do it. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about once you captured a German soldier or officer?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of interrogations were there? And who--

speaker: interviewee
text: I talk to him immediately. I mean, when I took him out, I talked to him immediately. I told him right away, I'm Jewish. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they-- what kinds of questions did you ask them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, all the questions. We ask them, normally-- first of all, we start from what city he is, his name, his birth, whatever it is. After that, what his family doing, same thing as you ask. And done it slowly. You know, we don't move in right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we ask him when he was drafted to the German army and how long he was in school, what kind of division you belong, what is the name of the division, when the division was formed, how he came to this part, part which he is now, how many people he is running. Normally, we took-- mostly, we took officers. You understand?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they knew, if, if they don't take-- if they don't tell me the truth, I know more or less what division is in the front. I mean, I, I, I knew when they, when they changed the division. Also, we knew-- we was working on it, to catch the people from the new one. And we, we knew a lot of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: We, we write up everything, what everybody say. And we transfer him to the higher division, to [NON-ENGLISH] division. And one problem I got one time-- and I, I got a big [NON-ENGLISH] on it. I catch the General Felkers from 27 [NON-ENGLISH]. I did a big job. And I made it. And when I carry him, I, I drink a little bit too much vodka. And, and I'm not joking. And, and I was on a horse. You know, he was going. And I was on the horse. I took off his, his boots. And I put it on, on myself. He has got tremendous boots. And it fit in my, my, my things. And when I came to the [NON-ENGLISH] division, I gave him over. And he was complaining, I must give him, I must give him back the boots.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was this general's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: General Felkers, from 27 [NON-ENGLISH]. He was, he was a big shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: A big German--

speaker: interviewee
text: He was, he was thinking I kill him. I said, I wouldn't kill you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what happened to him ultimately?

speaker: interviewee
text: He's still alive, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Living in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I think-- I think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: He would be very old, wouldn't he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was-- maybe he is not alive. I don't know. In this time, he was-- he was in the 30s, something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So just to clarify, for me, something, your job was more-- was much more reconnaissance than it was actual battle fighting?

speaker: interviewee
text: Only reconnaissance. I don't-- I don't-- I, I fight. If somebody hit me, I fight him back. But I-- only reconnaissance. That's all. I was an expert in reconnaissance and always that, that I did, nothing else.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of information could you or did you receive or find out from these German officers about what was happening to Jews? Or did you not even go into those questions?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't mention. The only person which I hit is when I took away-- when he was from the 16 Panzer Division, SS. And he was in Auschwitz. And he told me. And I find these, these, these pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you show us this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I find, I find these pictures, the whole things. This is the way I took it out from him. And-- and I don't want to say what I did with him, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, could you say what you did with him? I think--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing, I don't want to say nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are you sure? I think it's important to know that there-- I'll tell you why I think it's important. Because you, as a Jew-- because so many Jews couldn't fight and didn't fight. You were a person who did.

speaker: interviewee
text: I did, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I think--

speaker: interviewee
text: I fight plenty.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's so important.

speaker: interviewee
text: And, and I can tell you one more thing. The German armies, which they was-- knew me, I mean the name. I-- a lot of time, I came out to the front. And I through a horn, I tell them I'm Jewish. And my name is Alex Reifer. I told them everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: But getting back to this, this officer. Now, you don't-- do you remember this officer's name from the 16th Panzer Division?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I have it. But I don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: From whom you, you took this, this, these--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: This book, this little green book.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He was in Auschwitz. He was an SS in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: But now--

speaker: interviewee
text: For this, for this, he transfer-- they transfer him. I don't know. He did something wrong. I don't know what. He didn't tell me. And they transfer him to the front in the SS Panzer Division.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the Russian front?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. He must do something wrong, because if not, the big SS people did not transfer so fast.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I guess, because I think it's so heroic what you have done, that-- and it's so rare, that if you-- to tell us what you did to this soldier would be, I think, very important as a historic document to see that a, that a Jew was able to, to have the power in his own hands.

speaker: interviewee
text: I killed him. What should I tell you? That's all that I did. I couldn't stand it any more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- how did you kill him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, with a bullet [INAUDIBLE]. I kill many of them. He was not the only one. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you killed them, were you always face to face? Was it something you could see or close, as close as I am to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is the way it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there ever any remorse on your part?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nothing at all. Maybe he killed my family. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm glad you got him.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got one is nothing. I was supposed to get more. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the reconnaissance unit move east? How did you-- I'm sorry, move west. How did-- did you follow as the-- who went first, you or the fighting soldiers, since you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Fighting soldiers go first, always. When we move, you mean? You're talking about moving to a front? Normally, when we release, release somebody else, a different division is in front. And a different division comes down. Then in the meantime, this division which transferred, the other division, must go in before. And the other one leave later. I mean, they changing. Each part-- each, each battalion-- each battalion have several, several groups. And each group is a special design what to do, how to do it, how to-- which part of the, of the front to take over. Each battalion has a certain part. Everything is, is marked down. You couldn't jump in and take it. Everything is marked down exactly before we move.

speaker: interviewer
text: I need to go back, because something I forgot to ask you. And I know this is difficult. But if you could again hold up the, the-- I guess you would call it a wallet? What would you-- a photo album?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a small photo album.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're not going to look at what's in it now. But could you tell us what is in it?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is, this is pictures. He didn't say he took it. But I think he took it, because if not I would kill him twice. I only killed him one time. And-- [LAUGHS] and this is pictures from Auschwitz, from Treblinka, from people which-- nobody can take pictures down there. They don't take in a person like he, this gentleman which is taking now the pictures, to take pictures down there from dead people. This is a person which was working inside.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hold on to this thought. We're going to change the reel, OK?
